_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Tyrrell County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history. Somerset Place plantation records.
display_name: Tyrrell County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced over time. Records transferred. Columbia has been the county seat since the 18th century.

record_losses.description: Tyrrell County's records have survived intact. The county's extreme isolation — surrounded by swamp and reachable only by boat for most of its history — kept it beyond the reach of Civil War military action. County records date from formation in 1729.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Tyrrell County's courthouse records survive from 1729, but the county's tiny population and extreme poverty mean that many residents never generated documents. For {ancestor_name}, the records may be thin not because they were lost but because they were never created.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh, 2001.

deeds.gaps: Some early deed books show water damage. No major gaps.
deeds.repository: Tyrrell County Register of Deeds, Columbia; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records from the mid-18th century
wills_estates.gaps: Relatively few estates filed — the county's population was always small and many families owned little worth probating.
wills_estates.repository: Tyrrell County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

marriages.repository: Tyrrell County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court minutes from the 1730s
court_minutes.gaps: Some gaps in the earliest decades.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Tyrrell County Clerk of Court

vital_records.completeness: Very spotty until the 1930s. Extreme rural isolation meant many events went unregistered.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Somerset Place, on the shore of Lake Phelps, was one of the largest plantations in NC. The Collins family papers, held at the NC State Archives, document the enslaved community in unusual detail — births, deaths, labor assignments, material provisions, and family relationships for over 300 enslaved people.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives (Collins Family Papers); Somerset Place State Historic Site
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Extraordinary for enslaved families. The Collins papers are among the most complete plantation records in the state. Dorothy Spruill Redford's Somerset Homecoming (1988) demonstrated how these records can reconnect descendants with their enslaved ancestors. Birth and death registers, task assignments, and provision lists name individuals.
substitute_sources.source: Redford, Dorothy Spruill. Somerset Homecoming. New York: Doubleday, 1988.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Tyrrell County enumerated from the first federal census. Very small population makes tracking feasible.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential. The county's small population (under 5,000 through most of its history) means the entire county can be reviewed in a single census year.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Tyrrell County, NC.

substitute_sources.description: The Freedmen's Bureau operated in Tyrrell County after the war, documenting labor contracts and family relationships for the formerly enslaved population — many of them from Somerset Place.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for Black families, especially those from Somerset Place. Labor contracts name both employer and laborer, often with family members listed.
substitute_sources.source: Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands. National Archives, RG 105.

narrative_hooks: Tyrrell County's records survive from the 1730s, but the county was so small and so poor that many families left almost no paper trail — the absence of records here often reflects poverty, not destruction.

If {ancestor_name} was enslaved at Somerset Place, the Collins family papers at the NC State Archives are among the most detailed plantation records in North Carolina — births, deaths, labor assignments, and provision lists that name individuals by name.

Dorothy Spruill Redford's Somerset Homecoming showed what is possible when plantation records survive — if {ancestor_name} was among the 300 enslaved people at Somerset Place, the documentary trail may be richer than for almost any enslaved person in the Albemarle.
Tyrrell County's vital records before the 1930s are extremely thin — in a county reachable only by boat, births and deaths often went unreported. Census data is the most reliable substitute.

If {ancestor_name} disappeared from Tyrrell County after 1865, check the Roanoke Island Freedmen's Colony records — many formerly enslaved people from the Albemarle's interior counties fled to the colony during the war.
source_references: Redford, Dorothy Spruill. Somerset Homecoming: Recovering a Lost Heritage. New York: Doubleday, 1988.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
NC State Archives. Collins Family Papers (Somerset Place plantation records).
Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands. National Archives, RG 105.
National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Tyrrell County, NC.
